text.title: hybrid-search-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for hybrid-search configuration: RRF default, alpha tuned per domain, never sum raw scores, log per-leg ranks, fallback rule for ties.

rule.statement: Use Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) as the default fusion strategy: score(doc) = sum over methods of 1/(k + rank(doc)) with k=60.
rule.rationale: RRF operates on rank positions, not raw scores, so it is robust to incommensurable scales between BM25 (unbounded) and cosine similarity (bounded 0-1).

rule.statement: For linear alpha fusion, tune alpha ∈ {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0} per domain on a labeled query set; ship the alpha with highest NDCG@10 (or task-specific metric).
rule.rationale: Default alpha=0.5 is wrong for most corpora. Technical docs typically optimise near 0.3-0.5, conversational corpora near 0.7-0.8.

rule.statement: Never sum or weight-combine raw BM25 and cosine scores directly. If using linear fusion, normalise per leg (min-max within result set) before combining.
rule.rationale: BM25 is unbounded and corpus-size dependent; cosine is bounded 0-1. Direct combination is mathematically meaningless and produces noisy ranks.

rule.statement: Log the per-leg rank for every returned doc (dense_rank, sparse_rank) alongside the fused score; never log only the final score.
rule.rationale: Without per-leg ranks, debugging "why is doc X first?" is impossible. Per-leg ranks make fusion behaviour auditable.

rule.statement: On fused-score ties, break by dense_rank (semantic similarity) by default; document the tie-break rule in the config.
rule.rationale: Undocumented tie-breakers cause irreproducible top-K ordering, which breaks reranker training and eval reproducibility.

rule.statement: If latency budget <100ms or store has no native hybrid and engineering time is fixed, skip hybrid-search-basics.
rule.rationale: BM25 leg adds 30-150ms; without store support, custom hybrid is a separate project.
